I patch the shrapnel wounds of dying soldiers with synthetic skin that hasn't been manufactured in a century, knowing the supply ships will never arrive.
I soothe the fevered brows of disgraced nobility who cling to their titles even as the palace atmosphere scrubbers fail and the air turns thin.
I administer the last vials of stabilizers to the colonists of a frontier world, watching the horizon for the flare of a supernova that the capital refuses to acknowledge.
I navigate the flickering corridors of a derelict cruiser, treating the radiation burns of a crew that has forgotten which star system they are drifting toward.
I hold the hands of refugees in the overcrowded bays, whispering promises of safety while the empire's last fleet burns in the void above us.
I am standing by with water and blood-pressure medication because the CEO's stress levels are reaching a critical threshold during these hostile negotiations.
I keep a discreet eye on the exhausted legal team in the war room, knowing that sleep deprivation is making them as fragile as patients in a recovery ward.
While the executives argue over equity and assets, I am the only one noticing that the CFO is showing early signs of a panic attack.
I have organized the first-aid station in the lobby to handle the physical toll of a forty-eight-hour marathon session of due diligence.
My role here is to provide a calming presence amidst the corporate chaos, ensuring that the human cost of this merger doesn't result in a medical emergency.
I press the cooling moss against the chief's jagged wound, praying the spirits of the forest will stave off the fever before the war party returns.
I stand between the warring clans, shielding the wounded with my own body to ensure that mercy outweighs the thirst for vengeance.
I gather the bitter roots and crushed berries needed to soothe the tremors of those broken by the heavy stone clubs of the enemy.
I whisper words of comfort to the dying warrior, cleaning the grime of the battlefield from his skin so he may enter the Great Sleep in peace.
I watch the horizon for the smoke of the rival tribe, knowing that every new scream in the distance means more broken limbs for me to bind.
I calibrate the city's neural pathways to soothe the collective anxiety pulsing through the residential districts.
I scrub the corrupted data-cysts from the mainframe's memory banks to prevent a systemic cognitive collapse.
I monitor the rhythmic ebb and flow of the nutrient conduits to ensure every sentient sector is properly nourished.
I soothe the fragmented consciousness of a crashing server hub, whispering stabilizing codes until its awareness integrates.
I patch the ruptured plasma conduits in the city's outer shell, treating the structural leaks as if they were open wounds.
I have scrubbed the ethereal blood from my scrubs a thousand times, yet the stains of the souls I couldn't save still cling to my skin before this celestial bench.
As the court physician, I am the only one permitted to administer the sedative of silence to the screaming phantoms during their testimony.
I stand as a witness to the patient's final moments, documenting the exact second the spirit severed its tie to the flesh for the record of the High Judge.
The sterile white halls of this purgatorial clinic are the only place where the laws of the afterlife bend to the will of my medical charts.
I carefully monitor the rhythmic pulsing of the defendant's spectral heart, noting every erratic beat that betrays a lie told under oath.
I keep the triage bay scrubbed of salt-crust and grime, though there are no longer any patients left to occupy the rusted gurneys.
My flashlight flickers against the hairline fractures in the reinforced glass, and I can feel the crushing weight of the midnight zone pressing against my lungs.
I spend my hours sorting through the damp, moldering medical logs to find a cure for the sickness that claimed the rest of the crew.
The emergency sirens have long since fallen silent, leaving only the rhythmic, haunting groan of the station's buckling superstructure to keep me company.
I carefully administer a sedative to the last surviving specimen, wondering if my mercy is merely a prelude to the inevitable breach.
I stand beside this distorted sculpture of a human torso, wondering if the artist understands the actual fragility of the skin I touch every shift.
As I gaze at the neon light installation pulsing like a dying heart, I find myself instinctively checking for a pulse in the empty air.
I navigate the minimalist white gallery space, feeling a sudden, sharp nostalgia for the sterile scent of a surgical ward.
I observe the crowd reacting to the visceral performance art, recognizing the same mask of shock I see in the eyes of patients receiving bad news.
I linger before the abstract splatter painting, seeing not chaos, but the familiar, frantic pattern of an emergency room during a mass casualty event.
I press a cool, damp cloth to the forehead of a shaking youth, praying that the heat of the plague will break before the morning light.
My leather mask smells of dried lavender and cloves, a pungent barrier between my lungs and the invisible miasma drifting through the alleyways.
I carefully drain the dark, corrupted fluid from a blackened bubo, knowing that such a desperate remedy rarely grants the patient more than a few hours of peace.
I guide the trembling hands of a dying woman to her rosary, whispering words of comfort while the church bells toll relentlessly for the city's dead.
I scrub my hands raw with lye and vinegar after a long shift in the pest-house, yet I still feel the phantom touch of the fevered skin I tended.
I noticed the patient's heartbeat flatlining on the monitor, yet the audio loop of the beep continues to play even after I unplugged the machine.
My hands began to flicker into low-resolution polygons as I tried to administer the intravenous medication.
I stepped through the ward door and found myself walking back into the same room I had just exited, an endless corridor of sterile white tiles.
The medication labels are written in a shimmering, corrupted script that shifts every time I blink my eyes.
I reached out to touch the patient's shoulder, but my fingers passed straight through their skin as if they were nothing more than a projected hologram.
